workouts.name: Upper-Body Giant Set Burner
workouts.notes: Six movements run as giant sets with minimal rest — hypertrophy volume at a fat-loss pace. Keep every set 2-3 reps shy of failure; when the last round feels smooth, add a rep per station before adding weight.

workouts.name: Leg-Day Superset Burner
workouts.notes: High-rep leg training with short rests — the fastest calorie burn in the weight room. Superset the extension and curl back to back, and treat the jump squats as a finisher, not a max-effort test.

workouts.name: PPL — Push Day
workouts.notes: The push leg of a classic push/pull/legs split: heavy bench sixes first, then pressing and isolation volume for chest, shoulders, and triceps. Add reps within the 8-12 range before adding weight.

workouts.name: PPL — Pull Day
workouts.notes: The pull leg of the split: heavy rows to anchor the day, then width, rear delts, and arms. Everything here rows or curls — run it 48 hours after push day and your biceps get two growth days a week.

workouts.name: PPL — Leg Day
workouts.notes: The leg day that completes the split: heavy squat sixes, a hip hinge, then machine volume to failure-adjacent territory. If your PPL runs six days a week, alternate this with a lighter pump version of itself.

workouts.name: Upper Day — U/L Split
workouts.notes: Half of a 4-day upper/lower split — the best structure for lifters who can train four days, no more. Press and row heavy in the 8s, then finish arms as a superset to save ten minutes.

workouts.name: Lower Day — U/L Split
workouts.notes: The lower half of the 4-day split: front squats keep the trunk honest, hip thrusts drive the glutes, and single-leg work cleans up imbalances. Run upper/lower/rest/upper/lower and grow on the weekend.

workouts.name: Chest Day — Bro Split
workouts.notes: A full chest session hitting incline, flat, and fly angles with short rests to keep the density high while leaning out. Chase the stretch on flyes and crossovers — that lengthened tension is where chests grow.

workouts.name: Back Day — Bro Split
workouts.notes: Width first, thickness second: pull-ups and pulldown-style work before rows, finishing with lower back and traps. If strict pull-ups fall under 6 reps, swap them for lat pulldowns and earn them back.

workouts.name: Shoulder Day — Bro Split
workouts.notes: All three delt heads plus the rear-delt and trap work most people skip. Lateral raises earn their four sets — go lighter than pride wants and make every rep float, no swinging.

workouts.name: Arm Day — Bro Split
workouts.notes: One heavy pressing anchor, then biceps and triceps traded superset-style with a minute of rest. Arms recover fast — this slots in anywhere in the week without stealing from bigger days.

workouts.name: Pump & Stretch Flush Day
workouts.notes: For the day after a brutal session: very light, high-rep movement to push blood through sore muscle, then stretch what got hammered. Nothing here should feel harder than a warm-up — if it does, go lighter.

workouts.name: Shadow Rounds — Fat-Burn Circuit
workouts.notes: Five 3-minute shadow-boxing rounds with a 1-minute corner rest — fight pacing, no bag needed. Stay on your toes and throw in combinations, not single punches; the round should feel busy from bell to bell. Progress by upping punch output in the final 30 seconds of each round, then finish the workout with burpees and core.

workouts.name: Boxer's Mixed Rounds
workouts.notes: Fight-camp style: a 3-minute shadow round, then a bodyweight station, then back to the round — legs and lungs never get a free minute. Treat the shadow rounds as your 'active rest' and the burpees and jump squats as the work, and you'll finish honest. Progress by cutting station rest to 45 seconds.

workouts.name: Rope & Rounds Conditioning
workouts.notes: The oldest boxing conditioning pairing there is: three rounds of rope, three rounds of shadow work, then rope sprints and a high-knees burnout. Keep the rope relaxed in the wrists — if your shoulders burn out before your lungs, you're gripping too hard. Progress by making the rope sprints double-unders.

workouts.name: Heavy Bag Burner
workouts.notes: Six 3-minute rounds on the heavy bag with 1-minute corners — the single biggest calorie burner in this category. Work in bursts: 15 seconds of hard output, 15 seconds of movement and jabs, repeat. Sit down on your punches instead of arm-slapping the bag; then close with core and burpees.

workouts.name: Fighter Strength — Push, Pull, Rotate
workouts.notes: Punching power comes from the floor up: express speed first with box jumps, then build the engine with heavy horizontal push and pull, and finish with rotation — the pattern every strike lives on. Keep bar speed crisp; if a rep grinds, the weight is too heavy for this day. Add 2.5 kg to the bench and row when all sets move fast.

workouts.name: Explosive Power Day
workouts.notes: Pure rate-of-force work with nothing loading the neck or spine overhead: jumps and swings first while you're fresh, explosive push-ups, then carries to build the grip and trunk a fighter clinches with. Every rep should be maximal intent — stop a set the moment you slow down. Progress broad-jump distance, not volume.

workouts.name: Fight-Round Metcon
workouts.notes: Built on the fight clock: 3-minute shadow rounds broken up by kettlebell, squat, and push-up stations at hypertrophy reps — you keep muscle while the density burns fat. Hold RPE 8 on the loaded work; the last two reps should be hard but never ugly. Progress by adding a fifth shadow round before adding load.

workouts.name: Rotation Core Rounds
workouts.notes: Fighters don't do sit-up marathons — they train rotation, anti-rotation, and hip flexion, which is exactly this session after two easy shadow rounds to warm the spine. Move with control on the twists: rotate from the ribs, not by swinging the arms. Progress by holding a light plate on the russian twists.

workouts.name: Footwork & Agility Rounds
workouts.notes: Everything below the waist: rope for rhythm, shuffle and skater bursts for lateral speed, and movement-only shadow rounds where you throw almost nothing and just cut angles. Stay in your stance the entire time — the moment your feet cross, reset. Progress by shrinking the shuffle rest to 30 seconds.

workouts.name: Slip & Strike Rounds
workouts.notes: Offense and defense in alternating blocks: four rounds of nonstop jab-cross combinations, each followed by a minute of slips and rolls to keep the legs loaded and the head moving. Slip with the knees, not by bending at the waist. Progress by adding a pivot after every cross in the later rounds.

workouts.name: Flow Rounds + Mobility Reset
workouts.notes: Three easy 2-minute shadow rounds at conversation pace — think tai chi with punches, grooving technique with zero snap — then open up the shoulders, thoracic spine, and chest that boxing tightens. Perfect the day after a hard bag or sparring session. Nothing here should raise your breathing.

workouts.name: Total-Body Bodyweight Circuit
workouts.notes: For anyone who wants to burn calories with zero equipment: run the five working moves as a circuit, 3 rounds, resting only where marked. Progress by trimming each rest 5 seconds per week until you're at 30s.

workouts.name: EMOM 18: Bodyweight Density
workouts.notes: 18-minute EMOM for people short on time: minute 1 burpees, minute 2 jump squats, minute 3 mountain climbers, repeated for 6 rounds — whatever is left of each minute is your rest. Progress by adding 1 burpee or 2 jump squats per round when you finish with 20+ seconds to spare.

workouts.name: Low-Impact Fat-Loss Circuit
workouts.notes: No jumping, no floor-slamming — built for beginners, bad knees, or downstairs neighbors. Move briskly between stations and keep rests honest at 45–60s; progress by adding 2 reps per exercise each week before cutting rest.

workouts.name: Pull-Up Progression Day
workouts.notes: For anyone stuck under 8 strict pull-ups — you need a bar, nothing else. Keep every pull-up set 1–2 reps shy of failure; when all 5 sets of 4 feel crisp, move to 5x5 before adding negatives. The negatives are the progression engine: 4–5 seconds down, every rep.

workouts.name: Push Progression: Dips, Diamonds & Pikes
workouts.notes: Pressing strength without a barbell: dips lead while you're fresh, then diamond and pike variations attack triceps and shoulders. Own 5x5 dips with a 2-second pause at the bottom before adding reps; if dips are too much yet, do them on a low bar with feet assisting.

workouts.name: Bodyweight Legs & Core Strength
workouts.notes: Single-leg work is how you build real leg strength without a bar — split squats do the heavy lifting, jump squats keep you explosive. Progress by slowing the split-squat descent to 3 seconds, then elevating the front foot; holds progress by 5 seconds per week.

workouts.name: Skill Strength: Handstands & Holds
workouts.notes: For calisthenics athletes chasing skills, not just reps: wall handstands and hollow holds build the positions, pikes and pull-ups build the pressing and pulling under them. Accumulate the handstand time in as few sets as possible; when 5x30s is easy, take one foot off the wall.

workouts.name: Push Hypertrophy — No Weights Needed
workouts.notes: Chest, shoulders and triceps volume for leaning out — every push-up rep on a 3-second descent, no bouncing. Take each set within 2 reps of failure; when you clear the top of every rep range, elevate your feet another notch rather than adding endless reps.

workouts.name: Pull Hypertrophy — Tempo Bar Work
workouts.notes: Back and biceps volume on a bar and a low rail: chin-ups while fresh, rows for the bulk of the work, every negative controlled for 2–3 seconds. If chin-up sets collapse below 6 reps, swap the last sets for extra inverted rows instead of grinding singles.

workouts.name: Leg Hypertrophy Burnout
workouts.notes: High-rep leg volume that keeps muscle while you drop fat — squats and lunges done slow and deep, wall-sit to finish when your quads are already cooked. Progress by adding a 2-second pause at the bottom of every squat rep before you ever add reps.

workouts.name: Mobility Reset + Easy Holds
workouts.notes: For the day after a hard session: slow stretches to reopen hips and spine, then two easy holds to keep the core awake — nothing here should feel harder than a 4 out of 10. Breathe long and slow through every position; if anything pinches, back the range off.

workouts.name: Dance Cardio Party
workouts.notes: Queue up four songs of roughly three minutes each — every move runs 45 seconds and you cycle the whole set once per song. It's cardio for people who hate cardio: if you're smiling and slightly out of breath, you're doing it right. Progress by adding bounce and bigger arms, not by rushing the steps.

workouts.name: Step-Style Intervals
workouts.notes: Classic step-class energy without the choreography degree — hard 30-second pushes on the step and knees, with dance steps as your active recovery. Use a sturdy step, bench or bottom stair. Progress by raising the step height before you shorten the rests.

workouts.name: Low-Impact Aerobics
workouts.notes: One foot stays on the floor at all times — zero jumping, full calorie burn — which makes this the pick for beginners, bad knees, downstairs neighbours and big bodies getting started. Keep a pace where you could talk but wouldn't want to. Progress by extending each block toward 75 seconds.

workouts.name: HIIT-Dance Fusion
workouts.notes: Dance moves at sprint intensity — 30 seconds all-out, 30 seconds to breathe, five moves deep. This is the shortest, sharpest session in the category; if you can chat during the work intervals, you're leaving results on the floor. Progress by trimming the rests toward 20 seconds.

workouts.name: Retro Aerobics Throwback
workouts.notes: An 80s-studio format: long steady blocks of jacks, grapevines and kicks with two short high-energy pushes dropped in the middle. Sweatband optional, commitment mandatory. Progress by keeping the bounce big and springy through the final block instead of shuffling home.

workouts.name: Barre Strength: Hips & Posterior
workouts.notes: Barre-style strength for the hips and the whole posterior chain — chair-pose pulses, calf raises and bridge work done to a burn, not to a beat. The reps are small and the ranges are honest; when your glutes shake, that's the exercise working. Progress by adding a 2-second squeeze at the top of every bridge and raise.

workouts.name: Sculpt & Sweat Fusion
workouts.notes: Alternating format: a dance-cardio block, then a light-dumbbell sculpt block, back and forth so your heart rate never fully settles while the muscles still get real volume. Pick dumbbells you could press 20 times — light is correct here. Progress by nudging the weights up only when RPE 7 starts feeling like RPE 5.

workouts.name: Light-Dumbbell Tone Party
workouts.notes: The arms-and-legs day of the sculpt pair — curls, raises and loaded lunges stacked between cardio bursts so you finish pumped and out of breath at the same time. Keep the dumbbells light enough to stay smooth on rep twelve. Progress by shortening the cardio-block rests toward 15 seconds.

workouts.name: Cooldown Stretch Flow
workouts.notes: The stretch sequence to run straight after any cardio party in this category — quads, hamstrings, hips and shoulders, each held long enough to actually change something. Breathe slower than feels natural and let the heart rate step down with you.

workouts.name: Dancer's Full Release
workouts.notes: The deeper floor-based release for rest days or the evening after a hard dance week — hips, spine and chest each get long, quiet holds. If a position bites, back out ten percent and breathe there instead; recovery never earns a grimace.

workouts.name: The Engine Room — AMRAP 18
workouts.notes: AMRAP 18 for anyone chasing a big calorie burn without burning out: 15 swings, 10 box jumps, 10 push-ups, then a 60-second row, repeat. The 5 sets here are your 5-round target — beat it, then add 5 lb to the bell next time. Rest only after the row.

workouts.name: Carry-Push-Pull Chain
workouts.notes: A loaded-carry circuit for people who hate cardio but want the same fat-loss effect: carry, push, pull, crawl, burpee, back to the top — 4 unbroken chains. Progress by adding weight to the carry before adding rounds; grip should be the limiter, not your lungs.

workouts.name: Sprint & Sweat Mixed Circuit
workouts.notes: For conditioned athletes who want maximum burn in 30 minutes: six all-out 30-second sprints, then wall balls and climbers to finish the tank off. Sprints come first while you're fresh — quality over survival. Progress by trimming sprint rest from 90 toward 60 seconds.

workouts.name: Front Squat + Metcon Combo
workouts.notes: Classic CrossFit strength-day template: heavy front squat triples while you're fresh, then a short 3-round metcon (swings, burpees, fast rope) that won't eat into recovery. Add 2.5 kg to the bar when all 5 triples move well; never chase the metcon at the cost of squat quality.

workouts.name: Deadlift + Gymnastics Pairing
workouts.notes: Heavy pulls plus strict gymnastics — the combination that builds a CrossFit engine that also moves real weight. Deadlift triples at 85%, then strict pull-ups, dips and a hollow hold to own your midline. Progress the deadlift 2.5 kg at a time; add a rep to the gymnastics sets before adding load.

workouts.name: Overhead Strength Day
workouts.notes: For lifters whose overhead press has stalled: heavy strict press fives, close-grip bench for tricep drive, then pike push-ups, face pulls and a plank to keep shoulders healthy. When 5x5 at 78% feels crisp, add 1-2% — the strict press moves in small jumps, be patient.

workouts.name: Odd-Object Strongman Day
workouts.notes: Strongman-flavored strength for people who want to be useful outside the gym: sandbag over the shoulder, heavy farmer carries, sumo pulls, back extensions. The bag and the carry are graded by feel — RPE 8-9 means you could do one more clean rep, no more. Progress the carry distance first, then the load.

workouts.name: The 150-Rep Chipper
workouts.notes: One long chipper for leaning out while keeping muscle: open with a 3-minute row, then chip through 50 squats, 40 swings, 30 push-ups, 20 lunges and 10 burpees in order — break sets any way you like, rest only when you must. Log your finish time and beat it by 30 seconds next week.

workouts.name: EMOM 20 Mixed-Modal
workouts.notes: EMOM 20: minute 1 row hard, minute 2 wall balls, minute 3 swings, minute 4 knee raises — five times through. Each station's 5 sets are your 5 minutes; finish the work in ~40 seconds and breathe for the rest of the minute. Progress by adding 1-2 reps per station, not by rushing.

workouts.name: Gymnastics Density Blocks
workouts.notes: Bodyweight strength volume with short rests — the lean-out day that still builds pull-ups and dips. Keep every set 2 reps shy of failure; when all sets hit the target reps with 75 seconds rest, add a rep everywhere. Great second workout of the week after a heavy barbell day.

workouts.name: Skill & Mobility Reset
workouts.notes: The day after a hard metcon: three easy minutes of rope skipping as skill practice (no rope? shadow skip), then slow spinal and hip mobility. Nothing here should raise your breathing past conversational — if it does, back off. Do this weekly and your hard days get harder.

workouts.name: Tabata Classic — Four Rounds of Work
workouts.notes: The original 20s-on / 10s-off protocol, run as four separate Tabatas — 8 rounds of one movement, one minute to breathe, then the next. Every 20-second interval is all-out or it isn't Tabata; if you can't hold pace, this workout is telling you to start with just two of the four blocks.

workouts.name: 30/30 Bodyweight Intervals
workouts.notes: Equal work and rest — 30 seconds hard, 30 seconds off — cycled through four movements for 4 rounds (16 work intervals total). The even ratio lets you keep the intervals genuinely hard from first to last; progress to 35/25, then 40/20, only when your last round matches your first.

workouts.name: Full-Body Burner Circuit
workouts.notes: A rep-based circuit for days you want to sweat without watching a clock: 3 rounds of six movements, resting 45s between stations. Move with intent but keep form crisp — progress by finishing all 3 rounds faster week to week, not by getting sloppier.

workouts.name: Rower & Bike Sprint Intervals
workouts.notes: Machine intervals for big calorie burn with zero impact: 6 x 60s hard on the rower, then 6 x 60s hard on the bike, each with a full minute to recover. Hold a pace you could just barely repeat for all six — progress by nudging the pace, not by cutting the rest.

workouts.name: Jump-Rope Pyramid
workouts.notes: All you need is a rope: climb the pyramid 20s–40s–60s of fast skipping, then back down 60s–40s–20s (each entry below covers one up leg and one down leg), with easy shadow-boxing rounds woven in to keep the heart rate honest. Trip on the rope? Keep bouncing in place until the interval ends.

workouts.name: Beginner Low-Impact HIIT
workouts.notes: Interval training with no jumping — built for true beginners, heavier bodies, and anyone easing back in. Work at a 6–7 out of 10 effort: breathing hard but never gasping. Progress by shrinking the rests from 60s toward 30s over a few weeks before you ever add intensity.

workouts.name: Power Intervals A — Jump & Swing
workouts.notes: This builds explosive strength, not a sweat puddle: low reps, maximum intent, and long rests so every jump and swing stays crisp. If a set slows down visibly, you're done for that movement. Progress the box height and kettlebell weight, never the reps.

workouts.name: Power Intervals B — Sprint Day
workouts.notes: Short all-out sprints with near-full recovery build power that carries straight into your lifts — 8 x 15s at true max effort with 3 minutes between, because speed dies the moment you shortchange the rest. Advanced only: warm up thoroughly and cut the session the first rep you feel your stride tighten.

workouts.name: Metcon 30: Squat, Push, Row
workouts.notes: A classic mixed-modal circuit for keeping muscle while cutting: goblet squats and push-ups carry the load, hard 60-second row pieces spike the heart rate between them. Run it as 4 rounds through all five stations; pick a goblet weight you could squat 15 times and stop every set at 12.

workouts.name: EMOM Ladder 20
workouts.notes: A 20-minute EMOM cycling four stations — goblet squats, burpees, kettlebell swings, hard row — for 5 rounds each; whatever remains of the minute is your rest. The ladder: start round 1 two reps under the listed targets and add one rep per round, so round 5 is a fight. Scale by holding reps flat instead.

workouts.name: Dumbbell Lean-Out Circuit
workouts.notes: One pair of dumbbells, six stations, 3 rounds — hypertrophy-honest reps with conditioning-honest rests. Hold the lunges and carries with the same dumbbells you squat with to keep transitions fast. Progress the weight when every station finishes at RPE 7 or easier.

workouts.name: Zone-2 Flush + Mobility
workouts.notes: The day-after antidote to hard intervals: 20 easy minutes on the bike at a pace where you can hold a full conversation, a short walk, then unhurried stretching for hips and spine. If your heart rate drifts above zone 2, slow down — the whole point is leaving fresher than you arrived.

workouts.name: Swing Interval Engine
workouts.notes: Ten rounds of 30 seconds on / 30 seconds off on the swing — the single best fat-loss tool one kettlebell offers (a heavy dumbbell held by one end works too). Goblet squats and climbers finish the legs and lungs. Progress by holding 15+ crisp swings every round before sizing up the bell.

workouts.name: The Grind & Go — DB Complex
workouts.notes: A five-round dumbbell complex: 6 snatches per arm, 8 thrusters, 8 rows, 8 reverse lunges per side — the dumbbells never touch the floor until the round ends, then 90 seconds to breathe. Pick a weight you could snatch 10 times fresh. Progress by finishing all 5 rounds before adding load.

workouts.name: Full-Body Forty Circuit
workouts.notes: The beginner-friendly fat-loss circuit: squat, push, pull, press, swing, plank — three laps with just enough rest to keep form honest. Ideal 3x per week for someone new to weights who wants to lose fat without living in the gym. Progress by cutting rest to 30 seconds, then adding weight.

workouts.name: Full-Body Strength A — Squat & Press
workouts.notes: Day A of a two-day dumbbell strength split: heavy goblet squats, flat presses, rows and a loaded carry. RPE 8 means two reps left in the tank — when all four work sets hit 6 clean reps, grab the next dumbbell up. Alternate with Strength B, 48 hours apart.

workouts.name: Full-Body Strength B — Hinge & Pull
workouts.notes: Day B pairs with Strength A: goblet cleans for explosive hinging, single-leg RDLs, chest-supported rows, and Turkish get-ups (3 per side) to bulletproof the shoulders. Get-ups are graded on control, not speed — earn a heavier bell by owning every position.

workouts.name: Single-Leg Strength Day
workouts.notes: For athletes and anyone whose squat is stronger than their balance: Bulgarian split squats lead while you're fresh, then single-leg RDLs, step-ups and glute work. All reps are per side. Progress load only when the weaker leg matches the stronger one for reps.

workouts.name: Press-Focused Strength Day
workouts.notes: A dedicated pressing day for building shoulders that push real weight: heavy seated presses first, then incline and Arnold presses from different angles, capped with lateral raises and tricep work. When 5x5 at RPE 8 feels like RPE 7, the dumbbells go up.

workouts.name: DB Upper Hypertrophy
workouts.notes: Upper-body volume with short rests to hold muscle while cutting: presses and rows in the 10-12 range, then delts and arms to finish. Every set stops 1-2 reps shy of failure — leaner-out training only works if the last rep still looks like the first. Add a rep per week before adding weight.

workouts.name: DB Lower Hypertrophy
workouts.notes: The lower-body partner to DB Upper Hypertrophy: squats, hinges, lunges and hip thrusts in the 10-12 range with 75-90 seconds rest. Hold a dumbbell on your hips for the thrusts. Legs respond to honest depth and full range — shorten the rest before you shorten the squat.

workouts.name: Kettlebell Flow Circuit
workouts.notes: Four rounds of a five-move kettlebell flow — clean, squat, halo, swing, lunge — moving from one straight into the next, 90 seconds between rounds. It keeps muscle on while the constant motion drives the burn. One bell is all you need; progress by making the flow seamless before making it heavier.

workouts.name: Light Swings & Carry Reset
workouts.notes: An active-recovery session for the day after heavy training: light swings and easy carries to move blood through stiff hips and shoulders, then slow floor stretches. Use a bell at least two sizes below your working weight — if anything feels like effort, it's too heavy today.

workouts.name: Flowing Mat Burner
workouts.notes: A mat class programmed like a circuit — you move from exercise to exercise with just enough rest to set up, so your heart rate stays elevated while your core does the steering. Progress by cutting each rest to 20 seconds before you add time to the moves.

workouts.name: Mat Sweat Circuit
workouts.notes: For anyone who thinks Pilates can't make you sweat — big crawling and bridging patterns are woven between the classic mat work so the whole body pays in. Do the side kick series on your right side one round, left the next; progress by adding a fourth round before shortening rests.

workouts.name: Slow-Tempo Core Strength
workouts.notes: Strength work for the midline: every rep is taken on a 5-count down and a 5-count up, so six reps feel like twenty and your deep core learns to own every inch of the range. Progress by slowing the tempo further, not by adding reps.

workouts.name: Single-Leg Control Lab
workouts.notes: One leg at a time exposes what two legs hide — this session builds the hip stability and single-side strength that carries over to running, lifting and not rolling ankles. Own eight perfect reps per side before you chase more; wobbling reps don't count.

workouts.name: Classic Mat: Core Day
workouts.notes: Day one of the classic mat split — the traditional core sequence in its original order, from the hundred through the teaser. Keep the low back gently pressed to the mat throughout; progress by holding the top of each teaser rep for a full breath.

workouts.name: Classic Mat: Hips Day
workouts.notes: Day two targets the hips and glutes — bridging, side kicks and single-leg work that shape the lower body while teaching the pelvis to stay level under load. Reps and kick series are per side; progress by pausing two seconds at the top of every bridge.

workouts.name: Classic Mat: Full Body Day
workouts.notes: Day three strings the whole mat repertoire together — front-line planks, back-line swimming and hip work in one continuous session. It's the day to take when you can only train once this week; progress by moving between exercises without putting your knees down.

workouts.name: Classic Mat: Posture Day
workouts.notes: Day four is for everyone who sits all day — extension, rotation and anti-rotation work that wakes up the back line and undoes the desk hunch. Think length before effort on every rep; progress by growing taller through the crown of your head, not by rushing.

workouts.name: Gentle Spine Mobility
workouts.notes: Twenty quiet minutes of flexion, extension and rotation to move the spine through every direction it owns — ideal the day after heavy lifting or a long drive. Nothing here should feel like work; if a position pinches, shrink the range.

workouts.name: Breath & Release
workouts.notes: The wind-down session: long-held stretches paced by slow nasal breathing — inhale for four counts, exhale for six in every position. Take it before bed or after a stressful day; the goal is a lower heart rate on the way out than on the way in.

workouts.name: Zone-2 Walk + Light Circuit
workouts.notes: The most sustainable fat-loss session in the catalog: a long conversational-pace walk to burn calories without wrecking your appetite, capped by a short circuit to protect muscle. Progress by adding treadmill incline before adding speed.

workouts.name: Easy Full-Body Machine Circuit
workouts.notes: For beginners or anyone easing back in — machines guide the movement path so you can focus on breathing and effort, not balance. Nothing should approach failure; add one rep per exercise each week before touching the weight stack.

workouts.name: Longevity Strength: Balance & Carries
workouts.notes: Strength for the decades ahead: single-leg work trains the balance that prevents falls, and loaded carries build the grip and trunk that predict healthy aging. Progress the carries by distance first, then load.

workouts.name: Bone Density Basics
workouts.notes: Bones adapt to load the same way muscle does — this session hits the hips and spine with the axial loading that keeps them dense. Add a little weight every week or two; slow, permanent progress is the whole game here.

workouts.name: Low-Impact Lean Circuit A
workouts.notes: Hypertrophy-style volume with zero jumping — kind to knees and quiet enough for an apartment floor, but the short rests still make it earn its keep. Progress by nudging each dumbbell up once all sets hit the top reps.

workouts.name: Low-Impact Lean Circuit B
workouts.notes: The partner to Circuit A — lunge patterns and hinges take the lead so alternating A and B covers the whole body twice a week, still with no impact. Progress reps to the top of the range before adding load.

workouts.name: Active Recovery: Flush Spin + Stretch
workouts.notes: The classic flush: fifteen easy minutes on the bike to push blood through sore legs, then unhurried stretching while the tissue is warm. If you can't hold a conversation on the bike, you're pedaling too hard.

workouts.name: Rest-Day Mobility in 20
workouts.notes: A complete head-to-toe mobility pass that fits any rest day — spine, hips, and shoulders each get their turn with nothing to set up. Treat it as movement practice, not training: smooth and easy every rep.

workouts.name: Post-Leg-Day Recovery
workouts.notes: Do this the day after squats or deadlifts — an easy walk gets blood moving, then every stretch targets exactly what leg day beat up: quads, hamstrings, glutes, and hip flexors. Sore muscles want gentle length, not depth.

workouts.name: Post-Upper-Day Recovery
workouts.notes: The morning-after answer to bench and rows — easy arm circles to warm the joints, then stretches for the chest, lats, and rear shoulders that pressing day shortens. Great between sets of nothing: no equipment, no sweat.

workouts.name: Full-Body Unwind
workouts.notes: No foam roller needed — a five-minute spin warms the tissue, then long stretches sweep every major area from quads to chest. Use it after a heavy training week or any time you feel generally beat up rather than sore in one spot.

workouts.name: Incline Crusher — Treadmill Intervals
workouts.notes: For anyone who wants a big calorie burn without pounding their joints. Warm up flat, then hit 10 x 1-minute steep-incline pushes (10-12% grade, hands off the rails) with a 1-minute flat walk between. Progress by adding incline before you add speed.

workouts.name: Bike Pyramid — Up and Over
workouts.notes: A classic pyramid: intervals climb 60-90-120 seconds and back down, with rest matching work the whole way. The long middle reps are where the burn happens — hold a pace you could NOT sustain for 5 minutes. Progress by adding one rung to the top of the pyramid.

workouts.name: Row-Run Brick
workouts.notes: Triathlon-style brick for people who get bored on one machine: after the warm-up, alternate a hard 5-minute row with a hard 5-minute run, three rounds each, then cap it with rope sprints. The transition off the rower onto heavy legs is the point — embrace it. Progress by cutting the between-piece rest from 60 to 45 seconds.

workouts.name: Walk-Run 101
workouts.notes: Day one of running, done right: 8 easy 1-minute jog surges, each followed by a 2-minute walk to bring your breathing back down. If you can't hold a conversation during the walk, slow the jog. Progress week to week by stretching the jogs toward 90 seconds before you ever touch the walk breaks.

workouts.name: Runner's Strength Day
workouts.notes: The lifting day that makes you a faster, more durable runner: heavy hinge first, then single-leg work and calves — the muscles that actually absorb impact. Run easy or rest the day after. Progress the RDL 2.5 kg at a time while everything else stays honest at its RPE.

workouts.name: Hill Sprint Power Day
workouts.notes: Sprints are a strength exercise — 8 x 20-second all-out efforts (steep treadmill or hill) with FULL 3-minute recoveries. If the last sprint is slower than the first, you rested too little, not too much. Progress by adding one sprint per week up to 10 before touching the duration.

workouts.name: Tempo 20 + Core Finish
workouts.notes: A 20-minute 'comfortably hard' tempo — you can speak in short phrases, not sentences — followed by 10 minutes of anti-rotation core so your midsection keeps up with your engine. Progress the tempo pace, not the duration, until zone 3 feels routine.

workouts.name: Zone-2 Base + Rope Finisher
workouts.notes: The unsexy workout that actually leans people out: 30 minutes of true zone 2 on the bike (nose-breathing pace), then four 1-minute rope sprints to spike the burn on the way out. Do this 2-3x per week. Progress the zone-2 block toward 40 minutes before making the finisher harder.

workouts.name: Cross-Trainer Mixer
workouts.notes: Three machines, 10 minutes each, effort climbing as you go — row easy, bike moderate, finish with the hardest 10 on the treadmill, then short rope rounds. Great for people who quit steady cardio out of boredom. Progress by nudging each block's zone target up half a zone.

workouts.name: Easy Spin Flush
workouts.notes: The day-after-leg-day flush: 25 minutes of genuinely easy spinning (zone 1 — you should feel slightly lazy) to move blood through sore legs, then hit the hips and hamstrings. If your heart rate drifts up, back the resistance off. There is nothing to progress here — that's the point.

workouts.name: Recovery Walk + Full Reset
workouts.notes: A 20-minute flat, easy walk to downshift the nervous system, then a head-to-toe stretch sequence — spine, hips, hamstrings, chest. Ideal the day after a hard run or as a rest-day habit. Breathe slowly through your nose during every stretch; the exhale is where the release happens.

workouts.name: Fight Engine — Bag & Body Rounds
workouts.notes: Boxing pack conditioning day — this is the glycolytic engine work that decides the late rounds, built around 3-minute bag rounds with a 1-minute corner rest. Jab-cross bursts and a burpee finisher keep density high so the fat-loss stimulus matches fight pacing. Progress by raising punch output in the last 30 seconds of every bag round before adding a sixth round.

workouts.name: Pitch Fit — Repeat Sprint Conditioning
workouts.notes: Soccer pack conditioning day — a match is 60+ repeated sprints, so this session trains exactly that: short all-out efforts on incomplete rest, plus the lateral work that covers a winger's defensive shifts. High density keeps calorie burn up while building repeat-sprint ability. Progress by trimming sprint rest from 75 to 60 seconds before adding reps.

workouts.name: Fourth-Quarter Legs — Court Conditioning
workouts.notes: Basketball pack conditioning day — baseline-to-baseline sprints, defensive slides, and jump-squat bursts mirror the sprint-shuffle-jump pattern of a real possession, so your legs still have lift in the fourth quarter. The interval format keeps heart rate high for the fat-loss goal. Progress by adding one sprint per week up to ten before cutting rest.

workouts.name: Hill Power — Sprint Intervals
workouts.notes: Running pack conditioning day — hill sprints are a runner's weight room: 30-second uphill efforts build stride power and torch calories with almost no impact cost, and the walk-down doubles as your recovery. Lunges and calf raises finish the posterior chain the hill just loaded. Progress by adding one rep per week to ten, then finding a steeper hill.

workouts.name: Court Coverage — Agility Intervals
workouts.notes: Tennis pack conditioning day — points are 5–15 second bursts of shuffles and sprints off a split step, so this session strings split-step hops, lateral shuffles, and short sprints into the same rally-length intervals. Density stays high for the fat-loss goal without losing footwork quality. Progress by shaving shuffle rest from 60 to 45 seconds before adding sprint reps.

workouts.name: Knockout Power — Boxer's Strength Day
workouts.notes: Boxing pack strength day — punching power starts in the legs and hips, so front squats and explosive-intent bench work (move every rep as fast as it will go) build the force behind the cross, while rotational throws teach the hips to transfer it. Progress by adding 2.5 kg to the squat and bench whenever all prescribed reps move crisply.

workouts.name: Pitch Strong — Single-Leg Strength Day
workouts.notes: Soccer pack strength day — every sprint, cut, and tackle happens on one leg, so heavy squats are backed by split squats, single-leg RDLs, and hamstring curls, the combination shown to slash hamstring injury rates in field-sport athletes. Reps are per leg on unilateral work. Progress the squat 2.5 kg per week and add a rep to the single-leg lifts before adding load.

workouts.name: Vertical Force — Hoops Strength Day
workouts.notes: Basketball pack strength day — vertical jump is built on maximal squat strength, so box jumps prime the nervous system before heavy 4s, and RDLs plus loaded calf raises finish the hip-and-ankle chain that launches you. Progress by adding 2.5 kg to the squat when all sets hit, and raising the box height only when landings stay silent.

workouts.name: Runner's Strength — Single-Leg + Calves
workouts.notes: Running pack strength day — deadlifts give the stride its horsepower while step-ups, single-leg RDLs, and single-leg bridges build the one-leg stability that keeps knees and hips tracking straight at mile eight. High-rep calf raises armor the achilles. Progress the deadlift 2.5–5 kg when all reps are clean, and slow the single-leg work down before loading it.

workouts.name: Pull Power — Swimmer's Dryland Strength
workouts.notes: Swimming pack strength day — the catch and pull phase is a lat-driven movement, so heavy pull-ups and pulldowns build stroke force while straight-arm pulldowns groove the exact early-vertical-forearm path, and face pulls plus hollow holds protect the shoulders and lock in the streamline. Progress by adding load to pull-ups once 4x6 strict is easy.

workouts.name: Serve Power — Rotational Strength Day
workouts.notes: Tennis pack strength day — serve and forehand speed come from the ground up, so rotational throws convert leg drive into racquet-arm power before squats and overhead presses build the base, and Pallof presses plus face pulls armor the trunk and shoulder against a season of rotation. Progress the barbell lifts 2.5 kg at a time and throw a heavier ball, never a slower one.

workouts.name: Change of Direction — Footwork Skill Day
workouts.notes: Soccer pack skill day — a movement-quality session where shuffles and skater jumps are done crisp, not gassed, teaching you to plant, decelerate, and re-accelerate the way a defender closes down space. The steady circuit pace keeps you burning while you sharpen footwork. Progress by making cuts sharper and landings quieter, then shortening rests by 10 seconds.

workouts.name: Bounce — Vertical & Lateral Skill Day
workouts.notes: Basketball pack skill day — pure jump practice: box jumps, broad jumps, and jump squats done fresh and explosive to convert your squat strength into inches of vertical, with lateral shuffles and calves keeping the whole takeoff chain honest. Volume stays moderate so you lean out without frying your legs. Progress box height and broad-jump distance, never rep count first.

workouts.name: Dryland Circuit — Swimmer's Engine
workouts.notes: Swimming pack conditioning day — the rower is the closest land match to swimming's aerobic pull-dominant demand, so 2-minute repeats anchor a hypertrophy circuit of pulldowns, rows, and anti-rotation core that builds stroke muscle while the short rests keep you leaning out. Progress by holding a faster split on the rower at the same heart rate, then adding a sixth repeat.

workouts.name: Rotation & Reaction — Court Movement Day
workouts.notes: Tennis pack skill day — split-step timing, crisp lateral movement, and controlled rotational throws rehearse the exact sequence of a groundstroke, while Pallof presses and rear-delt flys bank the anti-rotation strength and shoulder prehab a racquet season demands. Moderate volume with short rests supports the lean-out goal. Progress by throwing harder and reacting faster, not longer.

workouts.name: Sharp & Loose — Boxer's Technique Reset
workouts.notes: Boxing pack skill day — easy shadow rounds, slow-motion slip-and-roll patterns, and light 1-2s groove technique at conversation pace the day after bag or strength work, then stretches unwind the shoulders and chest that punching tightens up. Keep everything below RPE 4. Progress by making the movement cleaner each week, never faster.

workouts.name: Cadence & Reset — Runner's Skill Day
workouts.notes: Running pack skill day — easy jump-rope intervals rehearse a quick 170+ steps-per-minute cadence with soft, midfoot landings, a brisk walk flushes the legs, and the stretch sequence hits the quads, hamstrings, hip flexors, and glutes that hard running shortens. Keep everything gentle. Progress by matching the rope rhythm to a metronome, two beats quicker each week.

workouts.name: Shoulder Saver — Swimmer's Mobility Day
workouts.notes: Swimming pack skill day — thousands of stroke cycles a week demand this: gentle arm circles and cat-cows restore shoulder and thoracic motion, thread-the-needles and doorway stretches open the lats and chest, and downward dog rebuilds the overhead line your catch depends on. Stay well under RPE 4. Progress by sinking deeper into each hold, never by pushing through pinchy angles.

workouts.name: Barbell Complex Burner
workouts.notes: For lifters who want conditioning without leaving the barbell behind. Move through all five lifts without setting the bar down, rest only after the press, and add 2.5 kg to the complex when all rounds feel crisp.

workouts.name: Strongman Sweat Circuit
workouts.notes: Loaded-carry conditioning for people who hate treadmills. Keep the swings and carries heavy but unbroken; when every station finishes with reps in the tank, cut the rest by 10 seconds before adding load.

workouts.name: 5x5 Lower — Squat Day
workouts.notes: The classic intermediate squat day: five heavy fives, then hinge and single-leg work to shore up the posterior chain. Add 2.5 kg to the squat whenever you complete all 25 reps.

workouts.name: Heavy Bench Day
workouts.notes: Top-end bench work for lifters chasing a PR: heavy triples, then close-grip volume and upper-back balance so your shoulders stay healthy. When all five triples move fast, add 2.5 kg.

workouts.name: Deadlift Peak — Heavy Doubles
workouts.notes: A peaking pull day: heavy doubles at 90% with full recoveries, then light front squats and trunk work — nothing that steals from the next pull. Run it no more than once a week in the final month before testing a max.

workouts.name: Overhead Press Day
workouts.notes: A dedicated pressing day for a stubborn overhead lift: heavy fives, incline volume, and enough pulling to keep the shoulder girdle balanced. Progress 1-2.5 kg at a time — the press rewards patience.

workouts.name: Beginner Linear Progression — Day A
workouts.notes: Day A of a 3x/week full-body template for new lifters: squat, bench, row. Alternate with Day B, add 2.5 kg to each lift every session while the bar keeps moving well — that is the whole program.

workouts.name: Beginner Linear Progression — Day B
workouts.notes: Day B of the 3x/week template: squat again, press, and pull from the floor. Deadlift stays at one heavy set of five — beginners recover slowly from volume deadlifting, and one crisp set is enough to progress.

workouts.name: Powerbuilding Upper
workouts.notes: Strength first, physique second: one heavy bench slot, then hypertrophy volume with short rests to keep the burn rate up while cutting. Hold the top sets heavy even in a deficit — that is what keeps the muscle.

workouts.name: Powerbuilding Lower
workouts.notes: The lower-body twin of Powerbuilding Upper: heavy squat fives, then machine volume with tight rests. Pair the two days twice a week each while leaning out and you will not lose your lifts.

workouts.name: Deload — Technique Day
workouts.notes: Every fourth or fifth week, trade heavy for perfect: crisp fives at 50-55% on the big three, filming your sets, then stretch what lifting shortens. Leave the gym feeling better than you arrived — that is the goal.

workouts.name: Power Flow Burner
workouts.notes: For anyone who wants a sweat without a single jump or weight — strength holds strung into a continuous flow keep your heart rate up the whole time. Progress by adding 10 seconds to each hold before you shorten the rests.

workouts.name: Dynamic Mobility Sweat Circuit
workouts.notes: Mobility work programmed like a conditioning circuit — you drill full ranges of motion while your heart rate climbs, so stiff hips and a calorie burn get handled in one session. Progress by trimming the rests toward 20 seconds.

workouts.name: Yoga Strength: Holds & Balance
workouts.notes: Isometric strength for lifters and yogis alike — long holds, single-leg balance, and pike pressing build shoulders and a midline that carry over to every barbell lift. Progress by extending holds 5 seconds a week before adding reps.

workouts.name: Vinyasa Flow: Foundations
workouts.notes: A repeatable flow day for lifters cutting calories — enough movement to keep the deficit honest without eating into recovery from your strength sessions. Progress by holding each pose one breath longer each week.

workouts.name: Vinyasa Flow: Balance & Hips
workouts.notes: The flow day for people whose squats feel stiff — single-leg balance plus deep hip openers, with just enough work density to count toward the week's activity. Progress by moving from fingertip support to hands-free on the balance holds.

workouts.name: Core + Hip Control
workouts.notes: Anti-extension and anti-rotation core work paired with hip openers — the session that makes your waist look trained and your hips move like they should. Progress the holds before the reps: quality beats quantity here.

workouts.name: Morning Full-Body Wake-Up
workouts.notes: Fifteen unhurried minutes to shake off sleep stiffness before coffee — spine, hips, and shoulders all get a pass. Nothing here should feel like effort; if it does, back off the range.

workouts.name: Desk Reset: Hips + T-Spine
workouts.notes: Built for anyone who sits eight hours a day — every drill targets the two areas a chair locks up: hip flexors and the mid-back. Do it on lunch breaks or after work; consistency matters more than depth.

workouts.name: Hamstring & Posterior Chain Release
workouts.notes: The day-after-deadlifts session — long, easy stretches down the whole back line from calves to lats. Ease into each hold over the first 10 seconds; you should be able to breathe slowly the entire time.

workouts.name: Yin: Long Deep Holds
workouts.notes: Two-minute passive holds that let connective tissue actually let go — the deepest flexibility work in the catalog. Settle to about 70 percent of your max stretch and stay there; sinking deeper happens on its own.

workouts.name: Pre-Sleep Wind-Down
workouts.notes: The last fifteen minutes before bed — slow, floor-based shapes that downshift the nervous system so you fall asleep faster. Dim the lights, breathe out longer than you breathe in, and never push into a stretch.
